# Fusion Next-Level Upgrade Guide

**From Extension Runner → Secure MCP Tool Fabric**

---

## 0. Target State (What You Are Building)

After this upgrade, Fusion will be:

* A **policy-enforced MCP server**
* With **capability-scoped VS Code extensions**
* Supporting **tool facets**
* With **first-class LSP exposure**
* With **streaming, observable execution**
* With **predictable compatibility guarantees**

In short: *Fusion becomes infrastructure, not tooling.*

---

## 1. Add a Formal Capability & Trust Model (Non-Negotiable)

### 1.1 New module layout

```text
crates/
 ├─ policy/
 │   ├─ src/
 │   │   ├─ lib.rs
 │   │   ├─ capability.rs
 │   │   ├─ manifest.rs
 │   │   ├─ enforcement.rs
 │   │   └─ trust.rs
```

Add this crate as a dependency to:

* `vscode-runtime`
* `mcp`
* `fusion-cli`

---

### 1.2 Capability definitions (drop-in)

```rust
// crates/policy/src/capability.rs
use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
pub enum Capability {
    FilesystemRead,
    FilesystemWrite,
    NetworkOutbound,
    ProcessSpawn,
    CredentialRead,
    CredentialWrite,
    WorkspaceInspect,
    LspAccess,
}

impl Capability {
    pub fn all() -> Vec<Self> {
        use Capability::*;
        vec![
            FilesystemRead,
            FilesystemWrite,
            NetworkOutbound,
            ProcessSpawn,
            CredentialRead,
            CredentialWrite,
            WorkspaceInspect,
            LspAccess,
        ]
    }
}
```

---

### 1.3 Extension Capability Manifest

Stored alongside installed extensions:

```json
{
  "extension": "google.gemini-code-assist",
  "trust": "verified",
  "capabilities": [
    "NetworkOutbound",
    "CredentialRead",
    "WorkspaceInspect"
  ]
}
```

---

### 1.4 Enforced at runtime (hard gate)

```rust
// crates/policy/src/enforcement.rs
use crate::capability::Capability;
use anyhow::{Result, bail};

pub fn enforce(
    requested: &[Capability],
    allowed: &[Capability],
) -> Result<()> {
    for cap in requested {
        if !allowed.contains(cap) {
            bail!("Capability {:?} not permitted", cap);
        }
    }
    Ok(())
}
```

Inject this check **before**:

* Node API calls
* filesystem access
* network access
* credential reads

This is where Fusion becomes *safe*.

---

## 2. Headless Compatibility Profiles (Predictability Over Magic)

### 2.1 Define compatibility levels

```rust
// crates/vscode-runtime/src/compat.rs
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub enum CompatibilityLevel {
    Full,
    Headless,
    Minimal,
}
```

---

### 2.2 Attach compatibility to extensions

```rust
pub struct ExtensionMetadata {
    pub id: String,
    pub compatibility: CompatibilityLevel,
}
```

If an extension attempts to use unsupported APIs:

* `Full`: allow
* `Headless`: warn + degrade
* `Minimal`: fail fast

Expose via CLI:

```bash
fusion extensions doctor google.gemini-code-assist
```

This prevents “it activated but did nothing” hell.

---

## 3. MCP Tool Facets (Composable Power)

### 3.1 Upgrade MCP tool model

```rust
// crates/mcp/src/tool.rs
use serde_json::Value;

pub struct McpTool {
    pub name: String,
    pub description: String,
    pub input_schema: Value,
    pub facets: Vec<McpToolFacet>,
}
```

```rust
pub struct McpToolFacet {
    pub name: String,
    pub description: String,
    pub handler: Box<dyn McpHandler>,
}
```

---

### 3.2 Example: Gemini upgrade

Instead of one command → one tool:

```text
gemini.generateCode
 ├─ preview
 ├─ diff
 ├─ apply
```

Each facet becomes its own callable MCP sub-tool.

This enables:

* CI pipelines
* agent planning
* user approval workflows

No extension rewrite required — Fusion orchestrates it.

---

## 4. First-Class LSP as MCP Resources (Major Power Jump)

### 4.1 New MCP resource type

```rust
// crates/mcp/src/resource.rs
pub enum McpResource {
    Tool(McpTool),
    LspServer(LspResource),
}
```

---

### 4.2 LSP resource definition

```rust
pub struct LspResource {
    pub language: String,
    pub capabilities: Vec<String>,
}
```

Expose endpoints like:

* `lsp.diagnostics`
* `lsp.symbols`
* `lsp.references`
* `lsp.codeActions`

This removes the fiction that LSPs are “extensions”.
They’re services — treat them as such.

---

## 5. Streaming Execution (Production MCP Expectation)

### 5.1 Streaming response type

```rust
// crates/mcp/src/stream.rs
use serde::Serialize;

#[derive(Serialize)]
pub enum StreamEvent {
    Started,
    Progress { message: String },
    Data { payload: serde_json::Value },
    Completed,
    Error { message: String },
}
```

---

### 5.2 Extension execution now emits streams

```rust
tx.send(StreamEvent::Progress {
    message: "Calling Gemini API".into(),
}).await?;
```

This gives:

* real-time feedback
* cancellation
* observability
* agent-friendly behavior

---

## 6. Tool Registry Becomes a Graph (Not a List)

### 6.1 Registry upgrade

```rust
pub struct ToolRegistry {
    tools: HashMap<String, McpTool>,
    dependencies: HashMap<String, Vec<String>>,
}
```

This lets you:

* resolve tool chains
* detect cycles
* plan execution order

This is the backbone of agent orchestration later.

---

## 7. CLI Upgrades (Minimal, Powerful)

New commands:

```bash
fusion tools list
fusion tools inspect 
fusion tools run 
fusion tools policy
fusion tools graph

example:
fusion tools inspect gemini.generateCode
fusion tools run gemini.generateCode.preview
fusion tools policy gemini.generateCode

```

No UX bloat. All power exposed.

---

## 8. Migration Plan (Safe & Incremental)

1. Add `policy` crate
2. Enforce capabilities only in “warn” mode
3. Introduce compatibility profiles
4. Enable MCP facets for one extension (Gemini)
5. Promote LSP to MCP resources
6. Flip enforcement to “hard fail”

Zero breaking changes if done in that order.

---

## 9. What This Unlocks (Strategically)

After this upgrade, Fusion can:

* act as a **secure AI tool backend**
* power CI/CD pipelines
* serve as an agent substrate
* replace IDEs for automation
* integrate with QuantumSuite trust scoring
* operate in air-gapped environments

This is not “VS Code outside VS Code”.

This is **the post-IDE execution layer**.

---
